explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\int_{-1}^1 \sin ^5 x \cos ^4 x d x=0\\ &\text { Since it is odd function. } \end{aligned} $$

content: $$ \text { The value of } \int_0^{2 \pi} \sqrt{1+\sin \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)} d x \text { is } $$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \int_0^{2 \pi} \sqrt{1+\sin \frac{x}{2}} \\ & \begin{aligned} \int_0^{2 \pi}\left|\cos \frac{x}{4}+\sin \frac{x}{4}\right| & =\left[4 \sin \frac{x}{4}-4 \cos \frac{x}{4}\right]_0^{2 \pi} \\ & =(4(1)-0(0-4) \\ & =8 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \int_0^1 \log \left(\frac{1}{x}-1\right) d x=\int_0^1 \log \left(\frac{1-x}{x}\right) d x \\ & I=\int_0^1 \log \left(\frac{1}{1+0-x}-1\right)=\int_0^1 \log \left(\frac{1}{1-x}-1\right) d x \\ & I=\int_0^1 \log \left(\frac{1-1+x}{1-x}\right) d x=\int_0^1 \log \left(\frac{x}{1-x}\right) d x \\ & 2 I=\int_0^1\left(\log \left(\frac{1-x}{x}\right)+\log \left(\frac{x}{1-x}\right)\right) d x \\ & 2 I=0 \quad \Rightarrow I=0 . \end{aligned} $$

$$\begin{aligned} \because \text { Let } f(x) & =\left(1-x^2\right) \sin x \cdot \cos ^2 x \\ f(-x) & =\left(1-(-x)^2\right) \sin (-x) \cdot \cos ^2(-x) \\ & =\left(1-x^2\right)(-\sin x)\left(\cos ^2 x\right) \\ & =-\left(1-x^2\right)(\sin x)\left(\cos ^2 x\right) \end{aligned}$$

So, $\quad f(x)=-f(-x)$

So, $f(x)$ is an odd function.

Hence, $\int_\limits{-\pi}^\pi\left(1-x^2\right) \sin x \cdot \cos ^2 x d x=0$

$$\begin{aligned} \int_1^5[\mid x & -3|+|1-x|] d x \\ & =\int_1^5|x-3| d x+\int_1^5|1-x| d x \\ & =\int_1^3|x-3| d x+\int_3^5|x-3| d x+\int_1^5|1-x| d x \\ & =\int_1^3(3-x) d x+\int_3^5(x-3) d x+\int_1^5(x-1) d x \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left[3 x-\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_1^3+\left[\frac{x^2}{2}-3 x\right]_3^5+\left[\frac{x^2}{2}-x\right]_1^5 \\ & =\left(3 \times 3-\frac{9}{2}\right)^{-}-\left(3 \times 1-\frac{1}{2}\right)+\left(\frac{5 \times 5}{2}-3 \times 5\right) \\ & \quad-\left(\frac{3 \times 3}{2}-3 \times 3\right)+\left(\frac{5 \times 5}{2}-5\right)-\left(\frac{1}{2}-1\right) \\ & =\frac{9}{2}-\frac{5}{2}-\frac{5}{2}+\frac{9}{2}+\frac{15}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \\ & =\frac{9-5-5+9+15+1}{2}=\frac{24}{2}=12 \end{aligned}$$

Let $$I=\int\limits_2^8 \frac{5^{\sqrt{10-x}}}{5^{\sqrt{x}}+5^{\sqrt{10-x}}} d x$$ ..... (i)

Using property $$\int_\limits0^a f(x) d x=\int_0^a f(0+a-x) d x$$

$$\begin{aligned} & I=\int_\limits2^8 5^{\sqrt{10-(2+8-x)}} \\ & I=\int_\limits2^8 \frac{5^{\sqrt{10-10+x}}}{5^{\sqrt{10-x}}+5^{\sqrt{10-10+x}}} \\ & I=\int_\limits2^8 \frac{5^{\sqrt{x}}}{5^{\sqrt{10-x}}+5^{\sqrt{x}}} \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 I=\int_\limits2^8 \frac{5^{\sqrt{10-x}}+5^{\sqrt{x}}}{5^{\sqrt{x}}+5^{\sqrt{10-x}}} d x \\ & 2 I=\int_\limits2^8 d x \\ & I=\frac{1}{2}[x]_2^8=\frac{1}{2} \times 6=3 \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\int_{-2}^0\left(x^3+3 x^2+3 x+3+(x+1) \cos (x+1)\right) d x$$ is equals to
explanation:
Let

$$\begin{aligned} & I=\int_\limits{-2}^0 x^3+3 x^2+3 x+3+(x+1) \cos (x+1) d x \\ & I=\int_\limits{-2}^0\left[(x+1)^3+2+(x+1) \cos (x+1) d x\right. \end{aligned}$$

Putting, $$x+1=t$$.

Then $$d x=d t$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & I=\int_\limits{-1}^1\left[t^3+2+t \cos t\right] d t \\ & I=\int_\limits{-1}^1(2) u_{.}=[2 t]_{-1}^1=2[1-(-1)]=4 \end{aligned}$$

As $$t^3+t \cos t$$ is an odd function.

content: $$\int\limits_0^\pi \frac{x \tan x}{\sec x \cdot \operatorname{cosec} x} d x$$ is equals to
explanation:
Let $$I=\int_\limits0^\pi \frac{x \tan x}{\sec x \cdot \operatorname{cosec} x} d x$$ .... (i)

Using property $$I=\int_\limits0^\pi f(x) d x$$

$$=\int_\limits0^\pi f(0+\pi-x) d x \text {. }$$

Then, $$I=\int_\limits0^\pi \frac{(\pi-x) \tan (\pi-x)}{\sec (\pi-x) \operatorname{cosec}(\pi-x)} d x$$

$$I=\int_\limits0^\pi \frac{(\pi-x) \tan x}{\sec x \operatorname{cosec} x} d x$$ ..... (ii)

On adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 I=\int_\limits0^\pi \frac{\tan x(x+\pi-x)}{\sec x \cdot \operatorname{cosec} x} d x \\ & 2 I=\int_\limits0^\pi \frac{\pi \tan x}{\sec x \operatorname{cosec} x} d x \\ & 2 I=\pi \int_\limits0^\pi \sin ^2 x d x \\ & 2 I=\pi \int_\limits0^\pi \frac{1-\cos 2 x}{2} d x \\ & 2 I=\pi\left[\frac{x}{2}-\frac{\sin 2 x}{4}\right]_0^\pi \\ & 2 I=\pi\left[\frac{\pi}{2}-0\right] \\ & I=\frac{\pi^2}{4} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$[x]$$ is the greatest integer function not greater than $$x$$, then $$\int_\limits0^8[x] d x$$ is equal to
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } I=\int_\limits0^8[x] d x \\ & I=\int_\limits0^1[x] d x+\int_\limits1^2[x] d x+\int_\limits2^3[x] d x+\int_\limits3^4[x] d x+\int_\limits4^5[x] d x +\int_\limits5^6[x] d x+\int_\limits6^7[x] d x+\int_\limits7^8[x] d x \\ & =\int_\limits0^1 0 d x+\int_\limits1^2 1 d x+\int_\limits2^3 2 d x+\int_\limits3^4 3 d x+\int_\limits4^5 4 d x +\int_\limits5^6 5 d x+\int_\limits6^7 6 d x+\int_\limits7^8 7 d x \\ & =0+1[x]_1^2+2[x]_2^3+3[x]_3^4+4[x]_4^5 +5[x]_5^6+6[x]_6^7+7[x]_7^8 \\ & =0+1(2-1)+2(3-2)+3(4-3) +4(5-4)+5(6-5)+6(7-6)+7(8-7) \\ & =1+2+3+4+5+6+7 \\ & =\frac{7 \cdot(7+1)}{2}=\frac{7 \cdot 8}{2}=7 \cdot 4=28 \\ \end{aligned}$$

Let $$I=\int_\limits0^{\pi / 2} \sqrt{\sin \theta} \cdot \cos ^3 \theta d \theta$$

Let $$\quad \sin \theta=t \Rightarrow \cos \theta d \theta=d t$$

When $$\quad \theta=0 \Rightarrow t=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { When } \quad \theta & =\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow t=1 \\ I & =\int_\limits0^1 \sqrt{t}\left(1-t^2\right) d t=\int_\limits0^1\left(t^{1 / 2}-t^{5 / 2}\right) \cdot d t \\ & =\left[\frac{t^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}\right]_0^1-\left[\frac{\frac{7}{2}}{\frac{7}{2}}\right]_0^1=\frac{2}{3}[1-0]-\frac{2}{7}[1-0] \\ & =\frac{2}{3}-\frac{2}{7}=\frac{14-6}{21}=\frac{8}{21} \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\int_0^1 \frac{x e^x}{(2+x)^3} d x$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text { Let } I =\int_\limits0^1 \frac{x e^x}{(2+x)^3} d x=\int_\limits0^1 e^x\left[\frac{(2+x)-2}{(2+x)^3}\right] d x \\ =\int_\limits0^1 e^x\left[\frac{1}{(2+x)^2}-\frac{2}{(2+x)^3}\right] d x \end{aligned}$$

Here, $$\frac{1}{(2+x)^2}=f(x)$$ and $$\frac{-2}{(2+x)^3}=f^{\prime}(x)$$

We know, $$\int e^x\left[f(x)+f^{\prime}(x)\right] d x=e^x f(x)+c$$

$$I=\left[e^x \cdot \frac{1}{(2+x)^2}\right]_0^1=\frac{e^x}{(2+1)^2}-\frac{e^0}{(2+0)^2}=\frac{e}{9}-\frac{1}{4}$$

content: Evaluate $$\int_\limits2^3 x^2 d x$$ as the limit of a sum
explanation:
Let $$I=\int_\limits2^3 x^2 d x$$

$$I=\int_\limits2^3 x^2 d x=\int_\limits2^3 f(x) d x$$, where $$f(x)=x^2$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Also, } a=2, b=3 \\ & \begin{array}{l} \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} h[f(2)+f(2+h)+f(2+2 h) \\ \quad+\ldots+f(2+(n-1) h)] \end{array} \\ & \text { where, } n h=b-a=3-2=1 \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} h\left[2^2+(2+h)^2+(2+2 h)^2+\ldots .+(2+(n-1) h)^2\right] \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} h\left[2^2+\left(2^2+h^2+4 h\right)+\left(2^2+2^2 h^2+8 h\right)\right. \\ & \left.\quad+\ldots+\left(2^2+(n-1)^2 h^2+4(n-1) h\right)\right] \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} h\left[2^2 h+h^2\left(1^2+2^2+\ldots . .+(n-1)^2\right)\right. \\ & \quad+4 h(1+2+\ldots+(n-1))] \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left[4 n h+\frac{h^3(n-1) n(2(n-1)+1)}{6}+4 h^2 \frac{(n-1) h}{2}\right] \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left[4 n h+\frac{(n h-h)(n h)(2 n h-h)}{6}+2(n h-h)(n h)\right] \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left[4 \times 1+\frac{(1-h) \cdot 1(2-h)}{6}+2(1-h) \cdot 1\right] \\ & =4+\frac{2}{6}+2=6+\frac{1}{3}=\frac{19}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos x \sin x}{1+\sin x} d x$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text { Let } I & =\int_\limits0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos x \sin x}{1+\sin x} d x \\ & =\int_\limits0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos x(1+\sin x-1)}{1+\sin x} d x \\ I & =\int_\limits0^{\pi / 2}\left(\frac{\cos x(1+\sin x)}{1+\sin x}-\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}\right) d x \end{aligned}$$

$$=\int_\limits0^{\pi / 2} \cos x d x-\int_\limits0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} d x=[\sin x]_0^{\pi / 2}-I_1\text{ .... (i)}$$

$$I_1=\int_\limits0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x} d x$$

Let $$1+\sin x=t$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \cos x d x & =d t \\ I_1 =\int_\limits0^{\pi / 2} \frac{d t}{t}=[\log t]_0^{\pi / 2}=[\log (1+\sin x)]_0^{\pi / 2} \\ =\log (1+1)-\log (1+0)=\log 2 \end{aligned}$$

Now, from Eq. (i), we have

$$I=[1-0]-\log 2=1-\log 2$$

content: If $$I_n=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \tan ^n x d x$$, where $$n$$ is positive integer, then $$I_{10}+I_8$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$I_n=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \tan ^n x d x\quad \text{.... (i)}$$

$$I_{n+2}=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \tan ^{n+2} x d x\quad \text{.... (ii)}$$

Adding Eqs. (i) and (ii),

$$\begin{aligned} I_n+I_{n+2} & =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \tan ^n x d x+\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \tan ^{n+2} x d x \\ & =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \tan ^n x \cdot \sec ^2 x d x \\ & =\left[\frac{\tan ^{n+1} x}{n+1}\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{1}{n+1} \end{aligned}$$

Thus, $$I_n+I_{n+2}=\frac{1}{(n+1)}$$

Substitute 8 for $$x$$.

$$I_8+I_{10}=\frac{1}{9}$$

content: The value of $$\int_0^{4042} \frac{\sqrt{x} d x}{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{4042-x}}$$ is equal to
explanation:
Let $$I=\int_0^{4042} \frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{4042-x}} d x\quad \text{.... (i)}$$

Using the property, $$\int_0^a f(x) d x=\int_0^a f(a-x) d x$$

$$I=\int_0^{4042} \frac{\sqrt{4042-x}}{\sqrt{4042-x}+\sqrt{x}}\quad \text{..... (ii)}$$

Adding Eqs. (i) and (ii),

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} & 2 I =\int_0^{4042} \frac{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{4042-x}}{\sqrt{4042-x}+\sqrt{x}} d x \\ \Rightarrow & 2 I =\int_0^{4042} I d x \\ \Rightarrow & 2 I =4042 \\ \Rightarrow & I =2021 \end{array}$$

content: The value of $$\int_{-1 / 2}^{1 / 2} \cos ^{-1} x d x$$ is
explanation:
We have, $$\int_{-1 / 2}^{1 / 2} \cos ^{-1} x d x$$

$$=\int_{-1 / 2}^{1 / 2}\left(\pi / 2-\sin ^{-1} x\right) d x$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\int_{-1 / 2}^{1 / 2} \pi / 2 d x-\int_{-1 / 2}^{1 / 2} \sin ^{-1} x d x \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2} \int_{-1 / 2}^{1 / 2} d x-0 \quad\left(\because \sin ^{-1} x \text { is odd function }\right) \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2}[x]_{-1 / 2}^{1 / 2}=\frac{\pi}{2}\left[\frac{1}{2}-(-1 / 2)\right]=\frac{\pi}{2}(1)=\frac{\pi}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: Find the value of $$\int_0^1 \frac{\log (1+x)}{1+x^2} d x$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text {Let } I=\int_0^1 \frac{\log (1+x)}{1+x^2} d x \\ & \text { Put } \quad x=\tan \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \quad d x=\sec ^2 \theta d \theta \\ & \text { When } x=0, \theta=0 \\ & \text { and when } x=1, \theta=\pi / 4 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \int_0^{\pi / 4} \frac{\log (1+\tan \theta)}{1+\tan ^2 \theta}\left(\sec ^2 \theta\right) d \theta \\ & I=\int_0^{\pi / 4} \log (1+\tan \theta) d \theta \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ &=\int_0^{\pi / 4} \log \left[1+\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta\right)\right] d \theta \\ &=\int_0^{\pi / 4} \log \left[1+\frac{\tan \pi / 4-\tan \theta}{1+\tan \pi / 4 \tan \theta}\right] d \theta \\ &=\int_0^{\pi / 4} \log \left(1+\frac{1-\tan \theta}{1+\tan \theta}\right) d \theta \\ &=\int_0^{\pi / 4} \log \left(\frac{2}{1+\tan \theta}\right) d \theta \end{aligned}$$

$$\int_0^{\pi /4} {[\log 2 - \log 1 + \tan \theta )]d\theta }\quad \text{.... (ii)}$$

On adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} 2 I & =\int_0^{\pi / 4} \log 2 d \theta \\ & =\log 2 \int_0^{\pi / 4} 1 d \theta=\log (\theta)_0^{\pi / 4} \\ & =\log (\pi / 4-0)=\frac{\pi}{4} \log 2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad I & =\frac{\pi}{8} \log 2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The value of $$\int_{-\pi / 2}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos x}{1+e^x} d x$$ is
explanation:
We have, $$\int_{-\pi / 2}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos x}{1+e^x} d x$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\int_0^{\pi / 2}\left(\frac{\cos x}{1+e^x}+\frac{\cos (-x)}{\left(1+e^{-x}\right)}\right) d x \\ & \left[\because \int_{-a}^a f(x) d x=\int_0^a(f(x)+f(-x)) d x\right] \\ & =\int_0^{\pi / 2}\left(\frac{\cos x}{1+e^x}+\frac{e^x \cos x}{1+e^x}\right) d x \\ & =\int_0^{\pi / 2} \cos x\left(\frac{1+e^x}{1+e^x}\right) d x \\ & =\int_0^{\pi / 2} \cos x d x=(\sin x)_0^{\pi / 2} \\ & =\left(\sin \frac{\pi}{2}-\sin 0\right)=1 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \int_{-3}^3 \cot ^{-1} x d x=\int_{-3}^3\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan ^{-1} x\right) d x \\ & =\int_{-3}^3 \frac{\pi}{2} d x-\int_{-3}^3 \tan ^{-1} x d x=\frac{\pi}{2}[x]_{-3}^3-0 \\ & \quad (\because \int_{-a}^a f(x) d x=0, \text { if } f\text { is odd functions) } \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2}[3+3]=\frac{\pi}{2} \times 6=3 \pi \end{aligned}$$

$$\int_0^2\left[x^2\right] d x=\int_0^1\left[x^2\right] d x+\int_1^{\sqrt{2}}\left[x^2\right] d x+\int_{\sqrt{2}}^{\sqrt{3}}\left[x^2\right] d x +\int_{\sqrt{3}}^2\left[x^2\right] d x$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\int_0^1 0 d x+\int_1^{\sqrt{2}} 1 d x+\int_\limits{\sqrt{2}}^{\sqrt{3}} 2 d x+\int_{\sqrt{3}}^2 3 d x \\ & =[x]_1^{\sqrt{2}}+[2 x]_{\sqrt{2}}^{\sqrt{3}}+[3 x]_{\sqrt{3}}^2 \\ & =(\sqrt{2}-1)+(2 \sqrt{3}-2 \sqrt{2})+(6-3 \sqrt{3}) \\ & =5-\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \int_0^1 \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{1-x}} d x=\int_0^1 \frac{1+x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} d x \\ & \quad=\int_0^1 \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} d x+\int_0^1 \frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} d x \\ & \quad=\left[\sin ^{-1} x\right]_0^1-\frac{1}{2} \int_0^1 \frac{-2 x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} d x \\ & \quad=\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-0\right)-\frac{1}{2} \int_1^0 \frac{d t}{\sqrt{t}} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { put } 1-x^2=t \Rightarrow-2 x d x=d t \text { at } x=0, t=1 \\ & \text { and } \quad x=1, t=0 \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \int_0^1 t^{-1 / 2} d t \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2}+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{t^{1 / 2}}{1 / 2}\right)_0^1=\frac{\pi}{2}+1 \end{aligned}$$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { We have, } I=\int_0^1 \frac{d x}{e^x+e^{-x}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad I=\int_0^1 \frac{e^x}{1+e^{2 x}} d x \\ & \text { put } e^x=t \text { and } e^x d x=d t \text {, where if } \\ & x=0, t=1 \text { and if } x=1, t=e \\ & \therefore \\ & \Rightarrow \quad I=\int_1^e \frac{d t}{1+t^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad I=\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_1^e \\ & \Rightarrow \quad I=\tan ^{-1} e-\tan ^{-1} 1 \\ & \Rightarrow \\ & I=\tan ^{-1} e-\frac{\pi}{4} \end{aligned} $$

Divide by $\cos ^2 \theta$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \quad I=\int_0^{\pi / 6} \frac{\sec ^2 \theta d \theta}{\sec ^2 \theta+\tan ^2 \theta} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad I=\int_0^{\pi / 6} \frac{\sec ^2 \theta d \theta}{1+2 \tan ^2 \theta} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{1}{2} \int_0^{\pi / 6} \frac{\sec ^2 \theta d \theta}{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2+\tan ^2 \theta} \\ & \text { Put } \tan \theta=t, \sec ^2 \theta d \theta=d t \\ & \theta=0, t=0 \quad \theta=\frac{\pi}{6}, t=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \end{aligned} $$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text {  We have, } I=\int_0^{1 / 2} \frac{d x}{\left(1+x^2\right) \sqrt{1-x^2}}\\
&\text { Put } x=\sin \theta \Rightarrow d x=\cos \theta d \theta\\
&x=0, \theta=0, x=\frac{1}{2}, \theta=\frac{\pi}{6}\\
&\therefore \quad I=\int_0^{\pi / 6} \frac{\cos \theta d \theta}{\left(1+\sin ^2 \theta\right) \cos \theta}\\
&\therefore \quad I=\int_0^{\pi / 6} \frac{d \theta}{1+\sin ^2 \theta}\\
&\begin{array}{ll}
\because & I=\frac{1}{2} \int_0^{1 / \sqrt{3}} \frac{d t}{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2\left(+t^2\right)} \\
\Rightarrow & I=\frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{2} \tan ^{-1} \sqrt{2} t\right)_0^{1 / \sqrt{3}} \\
\Rightarrow & I=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left[\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}\right)-0\right] \\
\Rightarrow & I=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}\right)
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$

content: $\int_{-2}^2|x \cos \pi x| d x$ is equal to

explanation:
$$ \text { Let } I=\int_{-\pi / 2}^{\pi / 2} \frac{d x}{e^{\sin x}+1} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{-\pi / 2}^{\pi / 2} \frac{d x}{e^{-\sin x}+1} \\ & {\left[\because \int_a^b f(a+b-x) d x=\int_a^b f(x) d x\right] } \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &=\int_{-\pi / 2}^{\pi / 2} \frac{e^{\sin x}}{e^{\sin x}+1} d x\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\\ &\text { On adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get }\\ &\begin{array}{rlrl} & & 2 I & =\int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} 1 d x \\ \Rightarrow & & 2 I & =[x]_{-\pi / 2}^{\pi / 2} \\ \Rightarrow & 2 I & =\pi \\ \therefore & & I & =\frac{\pi}{2} \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { }\\ &\begin{aligned} \text { Let } I & =\int_{-5}^5|x+2| d x \\ & =\int_{-5}^{-2}|x+2| d x+\int_{-2}^5|x+2| d x \\ & =-\int_{-5}^{-2}(x+2) d x+\int_{-2}^5(x+2) d x \\ & =-\left[\frac{x^2}{2}+2 x\right]_{-5}^{-2}+\left[\frac{x^2}{2}+2 x\right]_{-2}^5 \\ & =-\left[\left(\frac{4}{2}-4\right)-\left(\frac{25}{2}-10\right)\right] +\left[\left(\frac{25}{2}+10\right)-\left(\frac{4}{2}-4\right)\right] \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =-\left[-2-\frac{5}{2}\right]+\left[\frac{45}{2}+2\right] \\ & =2+\frac{5}{2}+\frac{45}{2}+2 \\ & =29 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} I & =\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{1}{a^2 \sin ^2 x+b^2 \cos ^2 x} d x \\ & =\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sec ^2 x}{a^2 \tan ^2 x+b^2} d x \end{aligned} $$

Put $\tan x=t$

$$ \Rightarrow \quad \sec ^2 x d x=d t $$

When $x=0, t=\tan 0=0$

and $x=\frac{\pi}{2}, t=\tan \frac{\pi}{2}=\infty$

content: $\int\limits_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\tan ^7 x}{\cot ^7 x+\tan ^7 x} d x$ is equal to
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\tan ^7 x}{\cot ^7 x+\tan ^7 x} d x \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & \Rightarrow I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\tan ^7(\pi / 2-x)}{\cot ^7(\pi / 2-x)+\tan ^7(\pi / 2-x)} d x \\ & \Rightarrow I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cot ^7 x}{\tan ^7 x+\cot ^7 x} d x \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned}\\ &\text { On adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} 2 I & =\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\tan ^7 x+\cot ^7 x}{\tan ^7 x+\cot ^7 x} d x \\ & =\int_0^{\pi / 2} 1 d x=[x]_0^{\pi / 2}=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ \therefore \quad I & =\frac{\pi}{4} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$
